fact: April 2 is the 92nd day of the year, which means you've already lived through more than a quarter of the entire year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, earthworms typically wiggle up to the surface around this time after spring rains to mate and lay eggs in the warming soil.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are often at peak bloom around this time, dropping so many pink petals that entire streets look like they're covered in fairy dust.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around April 2, making it easy to figure out directions without a compass.
In Antarctica, this is when the sun finally disappears for months of total darkness, while up in the Arctic, animals are experiencing 24-hour daylight.
Maple trees in North America are typically pumping out their sweetest sap right now, which is why maple syrup season peaks in early April.
April 2 falls exactly two weeks after the spring equinox, meaning days are getting noticeably longer and nights are shrinking fast in the Northern Hemisphere.
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are usually fully awake and super hungry by now, spending their days eating after months of sleeping.
In many places, April 2 typically marks the beginning of tornado season, when warm and cold air masses crash into each other and can create spinning storms.
Dandelions are usually exploding into bright yellow blooms everywhere by early April, turning lawns into fields of tiny suns that bees absolutely love.
